name: Las Vegas
visaSummary: Travelers from Visa Waiver Program countries need an approved ESTA before flying, valid for stays up to 90 days; other nationalities require a B-2 tourist visa.
bestTimeToVisit: March to May and September to November for warm days without the extreme summer desert heat.
weatherSummary: Hot desert climate with scorching, dry summers (Jun-Sep, often above 40°C) and mild winters (Dec-Feb) with cool nights; humidity and rainfall are low year-round.
etiquette: Tipping is expected everywhere — dealers, bartenders, valet, and housekeeping (15-20%).
Smoking is still permitted on many casino floors.
Dress codes apply at upscale nightclubs and restaurants.
The gambling age is strictly 21+, enforced with ID checks.
Photography is often restricted inside casinos.

Tipping 18-20% is standard at restaurants and for taxi rides.

Personal space is limited on transit — crowding isn't personal.

name: Maui
visaSummary: Travelers from Visa Waiver Program countries need an approved ESTA before flying, valid for stays up to 90 days; other nationalities require a B-2 tourist visa. Hawaii is a US state, so standard US entry rules apply.
bestTimeToVisit: April-May and September-October shoulder seasons for good weather, lower prices, and thinner crowds.
weatherSummary: Tropical climate with warm temperatures year-round; a drier season (Apr-Oct) and a slightly wetter, cooler season (Nov-Mar), with conditions varying sharply between the windward and leeward coasts and by elevation.
etiquette: Remove shoes before entering a home.
Respect "aloha" and "mālama 'āina" — caring for the land — in how you treat nature.
Never take lava rocks or sand from beaches; it's considered bad luck and disrespectful.
Yield to Hawaiian cultural practices at sacred sites (heiau).
Use only reef-safe, mineral-based sunscreen, required by Hawaii state law near reefs.

etiquette: Practice strict Leave No Trace principles within the national park.
Store food properly at campsites to avoid attracting bears.

name: Goa
visaSummary: India requires an e-Visa for most foreign nationalities (excluding SAARC countries under separate arrangements), applied for online before arrival and typically valid for stays of 30-90 days depending on the category; the passport must have at least six months' validity.
bestTimeToVisit: November to February for cool, dry, sunny weather ideal for beaches and sightseeing.
weatherSummary: Tropical climate with a hot, humid monsoon season (Jun-Sep) bringing heavy rainfall, and a warm, dry winter season (Nov-Feb) that is peak tourist season.
etiquette: Dress modestly away from beaches, especially at temples and churches (cover shoulders and knees).
Remove shoes before entering temples and some traditional homes.
Nudity and topless sunbathing are illegal and culturally offensive.
Bargain politely at markets rather than aggressively.
Respect Goa's blended Hindu-Catholic-Portuguese heritage in dress and conduct at religious sites.

localTransportGuide: Renting a scooter or motorbike is the most popular way to get around Goa's beach towns, though an international driving permit and helmet are strongly advised. Uber and Ola operate in the main towns, and hired taxis are common for day trips to further-flung sights like Dudhsagar Falls.
packingNotes: Lightweight breathable clothing for year-round heat
Modest cover-up clothing for temple and church visits
Reef-safe sunscreen for beach days
Insect repellent for evenings near paddy fields and forests
travelTips: Rent a scooter for easy beach-hopping, but wear a helmet and carry an international license
Visit North Goa for nightlife and South Goa for quieter, upscale beaches
Avoid the monsoon season (Jun-Sep) for swimming due to rough seas
Bargain at flea markets but stay respectful
thingsToAvoid: Swimming during monsoon season when rip currents are dangerous and lifeguard coverage drops
Riding a scooter without a helmet or license — police checks are common
Public nudity or topless sunbathing, which is illegal
Overpaying unlicensed taxi drivers — negotiate or use ride apps

name: Kerala
visaSummary: India requires an e-Visa for most foreign nationalities, applied for online before arrival and typically valid for stays of 30-90 days depending on the category; the passport must have at least six months' validity.
bestTimeToVisit: September to March for pleasant weather, ideal for backwater cruises and hill station visits.
weatherSummary: Tropical climate with a strong monsoon season (Jun-Sep) bringing heavy rainfall, and a warm, drier season (Oct-Mar) that is the most comfortable time to visit.
etiquette: Dress modestly, especially at temples, some of which restrict entry to non-Hindus.
Remove shoes before entering temples and traditional homes.
Use your right hand for eating and for giving or receiving items.
Ask permission before photographing people or religious ceremonies.
Respect Kerala's high literacy rate and progressive social norms.

localTransportGuide: Hired cars with drivers are the most practical way to cover Kerala's spread-out attractions between Kochi, Munnar, and Alleppey; local auto-rickshaws and the Ola/Uber apps work well within Kochi itself. Backwater houseboats function as both transport and accommodation for the Alleppey/Kumarakom stretch.
packingNotes: Lightweight breathable cotton clothing for humidity
A light sweater for cooler evenings in hill stations like Munnar
Modest clothing for temple visits
Mosquito repellent for backwater and forest areas
travelTips: Book a houseboat with a reputable operator and check reviews, as quality varies widely
Visit Munnar's tea plantations early morning for mist-free views
Carry cash for rural areas and small vendors where cards aren't accepted
Avoid the peak monsoon months (Jun-Aug) if backwater cruising is a priority
thingsToAvoid: Swimming at Kerala's Arabian Sea beaches without checking for rip currents and lifeguard presence
Entering Hindu temples that restrict non-Hindu visitors without checking first
Drinking tap water — stick to bottled or filtered water
Overpaying inflated "tourist price" houseboat bookings without comparing operators

name: Delhi
visaSummary: Most foreign nationals need an e-Visa (apply online 4-120 days before travel; tourist e-Visa allows stays up to 90 days for many nationalities). Indian passport holders need no visa. Overseas Citizen of India (OCI) cardholders and PIO cardholders travel visa-free with lifetime multiple entry, provided their OCI card is registered/linked to a valid foreign passport — a common arrangement for NRI travelers visiting family.
bestTimeToVisit: October to March, when daytime highs are 20-29°C and skies are clear; avoid the pre-monsoon heat of May-June (highs above 38°C) unless staying mostly indoors/AC.
weatherSummary: Extreme continental climate: scorching, dry summers (highs 38-40°C in May-June), a humid monsoon July-September with intense but short downpours, and cool, foggy winters (highs 18-21°C, night lows near 5°C, with dense fog delaying flights in late December-January).
etiquette: Remove shoes before entering temples, gurudwaras and mosques; cover your head with a scarf at Sikh gurudwaras (cloth is provided if needed).
Use your right hand for eating, giving/receiving items and handshakes — the left hand is considered unclean.
Dress modestly at religious sites and in Old Delhi — shoulders and knees covered is appreciated, especially for women.
Bargaining is expected in bazaars like Chandni Chowk and Sarojini Nagar, but is not appropriate in malls or fixed-price shops.
Public displays of affection are frowned upon at religious and heritage sites.

localTransportGuide: The Delhi Metro is fast, air-conditioned and covers most tourist areas — buy a rechargeable smart card to skip queues. Auto-rickshaws (insist on the meter or agree a fare beforehand) and Ola/Uber are widely available; at the airport, use the prepaid taxi counter or Metro Airport Express Line rather than touts.
packingNotes: Light breathable cotton clothing for summer heat
A warm jacket and layers for foggy December-January mornings
A scarf/dupatta for covering shoulders and head at religious sites
Comfortable, sturdy walking shoes for uneven Old Delhi lanes
travelTips: Take an early-morning Gatimaan or Vande Bharat Express to Agra for a same-day Taj Mahal trip rather than a slow road journey.
Book Red Fort and Humayun's Tomb tickets online via the ASI website to skip ticket-counter queues.
Bargain firmly (start around 40-50% of the quoted price) in Chandni Chowk and Sarojini Nagar.
Avoid travel agents who approach you unsolicited near Connaught Place or Paharganj claiming "government tourism office" status.
Winter fog (late Dec-Jan) regularly delays or diverts flights — build buffer time into connecting itineraries.
thingsToAvoid: Unregistered "government emporium" touts near Connaught Place and tourist sites pushing overpriced tours.
Unmetered taxis/autos at the airport — use the prepaid counter or a ride-hailing app instead.
Drinking tap water — stick to sealed bottled or filtered water.
Walking alone in poorly lit backstreets of Paharganj or Old Delhi late at night.
Touts at New Delhi Railway Station claiming your train is "cancelled" and redirecting you to a private travel office.

name: Agra
airportName: Agra Airport (nearest major gateway: Delhi DEL, ~230km)
visaSummary: Most foreign nationals need an Indian e-Visa arranged online before arrival (usually entering via Delhi). Indian citizens need no visa. OCI/PIO cardholders enter visa-free with a valid, linked foreign passport, which is a common route for diaspora travelers visiting on a Taj Mahal trip.
bestTimeToVisit: October to March for cool, clear mornings that make Taj Mahal sunrise visits pleasant; avoid May-June when highs regularly exceed 40°C.
weatherSummary: Hot semi-arid climate: brutal summer heat (highs 40-43°C in May-June), a humid monsoon July-September, and cool, hazy winters (highs 18-22°C, chilly mornings good for sunrise photography).
etiquette: Cover shoulders and knees when visiting the Taj Mahal and other mausoleums out of respect.
Shoe covers or barefoot walking is required on the Taj Mahal's marble platform — carry socks if you prefer not to go barefoot.
Photography of the Taj Mahal's main mausoleum interior is prohibited; exterior photography is welcomed.
Bargain politely but firmly with marble-inlay and handicraft vendors near tourist sites.
Avoid touching carvings and inlay work inside monuments — oils from hands damage the stone over time.

festivals.name: Taj Mahotsav

Private after-hours Agra Fort tour with a historian guide
Fine dining at Esphahan

honeymoonExperiences: Sunrise couple's visit to the Taj Mahal
Candlelit dinner with a Taj view terrace
Sunset boat ride on the Yamuna near Mehtab Bagh
Couples spa at Oberoi Amarvilas

Street food trail in Sadar Bazaar

localTransportGuide: Cycle-rickshaws and autos (both CNG and diesel) are the main way to get around Tajganj and the old city — the area right around the Taj Mahal is vehicle-restricted, so the final stretch is on foot, electric shuttle or battery-rickshaw. Ola/Uber operate in Agra but are less reliable than in bigger cities; hiring a car with driver for the day is common for Fatehpur Sikri trips.
packingNotes: Socks for walking the Taj Mahal's marble platform
Sunglasses and a hat — glare off white marble is intense
Modest clothing for mausoleum visits
A light jacket for chilly winter sunrise viewings
travelTips: Visit at sunrise — cooler temperatures, softer light and noticeably thinner crowds than midday.
Buy Taj Mahal e-tickets in advance on the official ASI website; foreign tourists pay a higher fee but it includes a bottled water and shoe covers.
The Taj Mahal is closed on Fridays for those not attending prayers.
Hire a government-licensed guide at the ticket counter to skip unofficial touts and get accurate history.
Pair the Taj with a same-day Agra Fort visit — combined tickets offer a small discount.
thingsToAvoid: Unofficial "marble factory" tours that pressure purchases of overpriced inlay stone souvenirs.
Touts near the Taj claiming the ticket counter is "closed" and offering to sell tickets themselves.
Food and drink stalls with visibly poor hygiene right outside monument gates.
Scheduling only a few rushed hours in Agra — traffic and security lines make it easy to underestimate time needed.
Fake "official guide" badges — verify guides through your hotel or the ASI counter.

name: Jaipur
visaSummary: Most foreign nationals require an Indian e-Visa applied for online before travel. Indian citizens need no visa, and OCI/PIO cardholders travel visa-free with a linked foreign passport — a frequent arrangement for NRIs visiting Rajasthan on family trips.
bestTimeToVisit: October to March, with pleasant highs of 22-29°C, ideal for fort-hopping and rooftop dining; avoid May-June (highs 40°C+).
weatherSummary: Semi-arid climate with scorching summers (highs 39-42°C in May-June), a short but heavy monsoon July-September, and mild, dry winters (highs 22-24°C, cool nights).
etiquette: Dress modestly when visiting City Palace, Amber Fort and temples — shoulders and knees covered.
Bargaining is the norm in bazaars like Johari Bazaar and Bapu Bazaar; start at roughly half the quoted price.
Ask permission before photographing local people, especially women in traditional dress.
Remove shoes before entering temple sanctums; some, like Govind Dev Ji Temple, have specific darshan (viewing) timings.
Elephant rides up Amber Fort are controversial for animal welfare — many travelers now opt for the jeep or walking route instead.

localTransportGuide: Auto-rickshaws and Ola/Uber cabs cover the city well; the Jaipur Metro runs a limited line between Mansarovar and Chandpole, useful mainly for the Pink City area. For fort-hopping (Amber, Nahargarh, Jaigarh), hiring an auto or car for a half-day is more efficient than individual rides.
packingNotes: Light cotton clothing plus a scarf for sun and modesty at forts/temples
Comfortable shoes for steep fort staircases and cobbled bazaar lanes
A light jacket for cool winter evenings
Sunglasses and sunscreen for the strong desert sun
travelTips: Visit Amber Fort early morning to beat both heat and tour-bus crowds.
Buy the composite ticket covering Amber Fort, Nahargarh, Jaigarh, Hawa Mahal and other monuments for better value.
Skip elephant rides up to Amber Fort and take the jeep or walk instead for animal welfare reasons.
Sunset at Nahargarh Fort offers one of the best panoramic views of the Pink City.
Gem and carpet "cooperative" shops often pay commissions to guides/drivers who bring tourists — verify prices independently before buying.
thingsToAvoid: Gemstone "investment" scams where shops claim stones can be resold abroad at a profit.
Unlicensed guides loitering at fort entrances — book through your hotel or the tourism counter.
Overpaying for photos with snake charmers or decorated camels/elephants near tourist sites.
Walking through Johari Bazaar during peak evening traffic hours if avoiding crowds and rickshaw congestion.
Drinking tap water — stick to sealed bottled water.

name: Udaipur
visaSummary: Most foreign nationals require an Indian e-Visa arranged online before arrival. Indian citizens travel visa-free, and OCI/PIO cardholders enter visa-free with a linked foreign passport — relevant for the many NRI couples who choose Udaipur for destination weddings.
bestTimeToVisit: September to March, when lake levels are good and highs sit around 24-32°C; avoid May-June heat, though the lakes can look dramatic post-monsoon in September.
weatherSummary: Milder than the Rajasthan desert cities thanks to its lake-and-hills setting: hot but not extreme summers (highs 36-38°C in May), a good monsoon July-September that fills the lakes, and pleasant winters (highs 25-27°C, cool nights around 8-10°C).
etiquette: Dress modestly at City Palace and temples — cover shoulders and knees.
Ask before photographing locals, particularly at ghats where daily rituals and washing take place.
Remove shoes before entering the Jagdish Temple and other shrines.
Bargain politely in Hathi Pol and Bara Bazaar, but expect fixed prices at government emporiums.
Lake Pichola and Fateh Sagar are considered sacred by many locals — avoid littering or disruptive behavior on boat tours.

festivals.name: Shilpgram Utsav

Free sunset watching from Ambrai Ghat

localTransportGuide: Udaipur's old city is compact and walkable around the lakes; auto-rickshaws and Ola/Uber cover longer hops like Sajjangarh or the airport. Renting a scooter is popular for exploring Fateh Sagar and nearby villages independently, though traffic can be chaotic near the Clock Tower area.
packingNotes: Light layers — warm days but cool lake-breeze evenings, especially Nov-Feb
Modest clothing for temples and the City Palace
Comfortable shoes for palace courtyards and ghat steps
A light jacket for winter mornings and Sajjangarh sunset visits
travelTips: Book a sunset boat ride on Lake Pichola in advance during peak season (Oct-Mar) — slots fill fast.
Watch the nightly Dharohar folk dance show at Bagore Ki Haveli for an authentic Mewar culture experience.
Rooftop restaurants around Jagdish Temple offer some of the best value City Palace views.
Combine Kumbhalgarh Fort and Ranakpur Jain Temples into one full-day trip from Udaipur.
September, right after monsoon, offers full lakes and dramatic views with far fewer tourists than winter.
thingsToAvoid: Miniature-painting shops that push hard-sell "artist studio" tours with inflated prices.
Unlicensed boat operators away from the official Lake Pichola jetty.
Overpaying for scooter rentals without checking the vehicle for existing damage first.
Visiting Sajjangarh (Monsoon Palace) in heavy monsoon fog when the view is obscured.
Wandering isolated ghats alone after dark.

name: Jodhpur
visaSummary: Most foreign nationals require an Indian e-Visa applied for online ahead of travel. Indian citizens need no visa, and OCI/PIO cardholders can enter visa-free with a linked foreign passport, a common route for NRI travelers combining Jodhpur with a Rajasthan family circuit.
bestTimeToVisit: October to March, when highs of 24-30°C make exploring Mehrangarh Fort and the Blue City lanes comfortable; avoid May-June desert heat above 40°C.
weatherSummary: Hot desert climate on the edge of the Thar: intense, dry summer heat (highs 40-42°C in May-June), a light monsoon July-September, and mild, dry winters (highs 24-26°C, cold nights near 6-8°C).
etiquette: Dress modestly when visiting Mehrangarh Fort and Jaswant Thada.
Ask permission before photographing residents in the blue-painted lanes of the old city — these are private homes, not sets.
Bargain respectfully in Sardar Market and the spice market around the Clock Tower.
Remove shoes before entering temple sanctums.
Avoid loud behavior near cenotaphs (Jaswant Thada) which remain active sites of remembrance for the royal family.

attractionsPaid.name: Mehrangarh Fort
attractionsPaid.note: One of India's largest forts, towering over the Blue City

localTransportGuide: Auto-rickshaws and Ola/Uber operate throughout the city; the old Blue City lanes are best explored on foot since they are too narrow for vehicles. For day trips to Osian or Bishnoi villages, hiring a car with driver for the day is the practical option.
packingNotes: Breathable cotton clothing for desert heat, layered for cool winter nights
A scarf for sun protection and modesty at the fort/temples
Sturdy shoes for uneven fort ramparts and stepped lanes
Sunglasses for the strong desert glare
travelTips: Visit Mehrangarh Fort with the included audio guide — it is one of the best-curated fort audio tours in India.
Sunset from the fort ramparts over the Blue City is spectacular — arrive an hour before closing.
Toorji Ka Jhalra stepwell is quietest and most photogenic at sunrise.
Book the RIFF folk music festival dates in October well ahead if visiting for the fort concerts.
Combine a Bishnoi village safari with a stop at Guda Bishnoi Lake for wildlife spotting (blackbuck, chinkara).
thingsToAvoid: Overpriced "government approved" textile and carpet shops steering tourists via commission-earning drivers.
Walking through narrow Blue City lanes at night without a local guide — it's easy to get disoriented.
Camel or jeep safari operators without visible safety equipment or reviews.
Peak summer midday sightseeing (May-June) — heatstroke risk is real in the desert sun.
Buying "antique" miniature paintings at inflated prices without verifying authenticity.

name: Mumbai
visaSummary: Most foreign nationals require an Indian e-Visa applied for online before travel. Indian citizens need no visa, and OCI/PIO cardholders enter visa-free with a linked foreign passport — common for the large NRI diaspora with family or business ties to Mumbai.
bestTimeToVisit: November to February for cooler, drier weather (highs 29-32°C); the June-September monsoon brings dramatic but disruptive rain and is best avoided for sightseeing.
weatherSummary: Tropical climate moderated by the Arabian Sea: warm and humid nearly year-round (highs 29-33°C), with a very heavy monsoon June-September that can cause local flooding, and a relatively mild, dry winter (Dec-Feb).
etiquette: Dress modestly at religious sites such as Haji Ali Dargah and Siddhivinayak Temple.
Queue discipline on Mumbai's suburban trains is important — stand aside to let passengers exit before boarding.
Tipping 10% at restaurants is appreciated though not always automatically added.
Avoid discussing sensitive topics like the 2008 attacks casually at memorial sites such as the Taj Mahal Palace Hotel.
Remove shoes before entering temples and some traditional homes.

festivals.name: Ganesh Chaturthi
festivals.timing: August-September (per lunar calendar), Mumbai's biggest festival

localTransportGuide: The suburban local train network is the fastest way across the city but gets extremely crowded at rush hour; the Mumbai Metro and monorail cover newer corridors. Ola/Uber and black-and-yellow metered taxis are widely available for point-to-point travel, and a ferry connects the Gateway of India to Elephanta Caves.
packingNotes: Light, breathable clothing for year-round humidity
A compact umbrella or raincoat if visiting June-September
Comfortable shoes for uneven pavements and station crowds
A modest outfit for visiting Haji Ali Dargah or Siddhivinayak Temple
travelTips: Avoid peak rush hour (8:30-10:30am and 6-8:30pm) on suburban trains if carrying luggage.
Book Elephanta Caves ferry tickets in the morning — the last return ferry leaves mid-afternoon.
Bandra and Lower Parel have Mumbai's best contemporary dining and nightlife scenes.
Monsoon season (Jun-Sep) can cause waterlogging and flight delays — check forecasts before island-hopping day trips.
The Gateway of India area is best visited early morning or evening to avoid both heat and crowds.
thingsToAvoid: Unlicensed taxi touts outside the airport — use the prepaid counter or a ride-hailing app.
Swimming at Juhu or Chowpatty beaches — currents and pollution make them unsafe for swimming.
Walking on train tracks or overcrowded platforms — Mumbai's rail system has real safety risks for the inattentive.
Overpriced "Bollywood audition" scams targeting tourists in Bandra and Colaba.
Traveling during severe monsoon flooding alerts — the city can grind to a halt.

name: Varanasi
visaSummary: Most foreign nationals require an Indian e-Visa applied for online before travel. Indian citizens need no visa, and OCI/PIO cardholders travel visa-free with a linked foreign passport, common for diaspora visitors on a pilgrimage or heritage trip.
bestTimeToVisit: October to March, with cooler mornings (highs 22-27°C) ideal for sunrise boat rides on the Ganges; avoid the extreme heat of May-June.
weatherSummary: Hot continental climate typical of the Gangetic plains: scorching summers (highs 40-42°C in May-June), a humid monsoon July-September that raises the river level significantly, and cool, foggy winters (highs 22-24°C, cold mornings near 8-10°C).
etiquette: Dress conservatively — shoulders and knees covered — especially near the ghats and temples.
Ask permission before photographing cremation rites at Manikarnika or Harishchandra Ghat; many locals consider it deeply disrespectful to photograph the ceremonies themselves.
Remove shoes before entering the Kashi Vishwanath Temple; expect airport-style security and no phones/cameras inside.
Do not touch the Ganges water with your feet if others are performing rituals nearby — approach quietly and respectfully.
Alcohol and meat are frowned upon in much of the old city near the ghats.

festivals.name: Dev Deepawali
festivals.timing: Full moon night in November, thousands of lamps lit along the ghats

festivals.name: Ganga Mahotsav
festivals.timing: November, five-day cultural festival before Dev Deepawali

Walking tour of the old-city lanes
localTransportGuide: The old city around the ghats is a maze of narrow lanes only navigable on foot or by cycle-rickshaw; auto-rickshaws and Ola/Uber cover the wider city and the route to Sarnath. Boats are the standard way to move along the riverfront between ghats.
packingNotes: Modest, conservative clothing — shoulders and knees covered
Layers for cold winter mornings on the river
Closed shoes tolerant of uneven, sometimes wet ghat steps
A shawl or scarf for temple visits
travelTips: Take the sunrise boat ride first thing (before 6:30am) for the best light and calmest water.
Attend the Ganga Aarti at Dashashwamedh Ghat by arriving 45-60 minutes early to get a good spot, or view it from a boat on the river.
Kashi Vishwanath Temple has strict security — leave phones, bags and electronics at your hotel or in a locker.
Hire a licensed boatman through your hotel rather than the first tout who approaches you at the ghat.
Dev Deepawali (Nov) is spectacular but extremely crowded — book accommodation and boats months ahead.
thingsToAvoid: Photographing cremation ceremonies at Manikarnika or Harishchandra Ghat.
"Holy man" and fake priest scams demanding money for impromptu blessings or forced flower offerings.
Drinking or bathing in the Ganges water — it carries a high bacterial load despite its spiritual significance.
Unlicensed boatmen quoting inflated tourist prices — agree the fare before boarding.
Wandering narrow ghat lanes alone late at night.

name: Rishikesh
visaSummary: Most foreign nationals require an Indian e-Visa applied for online before travel. Indian citizens need no visa, and OCI/PIO cardholders travel visa-free with a linked foreign passport — relevant for the many diaspora visitors who come specifically for yoga teacher-training programs.
bestTimeToVisit: September to April for pleasant trekking and rafting conditions (highs 20-30°C); the Ganges is at its highest and most thrilling for rafting just after monsoon (Sep-Oct).
weatherSummary: Foothill climate milder than the plains below: warm summers (highs 32-36°C in May-June), a wet monsoon June-September that raises the Ganges, and cool, pleasant winters (highs 18-20°C, chilly nights near 5-8°C).
etiquette: Rishikesh is officially vegetarian and alcohol-free within municipal limits — avoid bringing meat or alcohol into ashrams and much of the town.
Dress modestly, especially around ashrams and temples — shoulders and knees covered.
Remove shoes before entering ashrams, yoga halls and temples.
Keep noise levels down near ashrams and the riverbanks, which are considered meditative spaces.
Ask before photographing sadhus (holy men) — a small donation is customary if they agree.

festivals.name: Kumbh Mela (nearby Haridwar)
festivals.timing: Once every 12 years, with smaller Ardh Kumbh every 6 years

adventureActivities: Bungee jumping and giant swing at Jumpin Heights
Cliff jumping and camping on Ganges riverside beaches

Private yoga and meditation retreat with a personal instructor
Ayurvedic spa and panchakarma treatment package
Private sunrise rafting charter
honeymoonExperiences: Sunset walk across Laxman Jhula together
Couple's yoga and meditation session
Riverside candlelight dinner at a boutique resort
Evening aarti followed by a quiet riverside stroll

Budget ashram stay with included yoga classes
Walking the free Laxman Jhula and Ram Jhula circuit
Shared-cost rafting trip with a small group
parks: Rajaji National Park (nearby, tiger and elephant reserve)
Terraced riverside gardens at Parmarth Niketan
beaches: Ganges riverside sand beaches near Ram Jhula (river beaches, not sea)
localTransportGuide: Rishikesh is walkable along the two main riverside hubs (Laxman Jhula and Ram Jhula), connected by shared autos and e-rickshaws; scooters are popular rentals for reaching Neelkanth Temple or waterfalls. Vehicles cannot cross the pedestrian suspension bridges, so plan foot time on both banks.
packingNotes: Modest, loose-fitting clothing suitable for yoga and ashram visits
Quick-dry clothes and sandals for rafting
A light jacket for cool riverside evenings
Reusable water bottle — plastic bottles are discouraged near the river
travelTips: Book rafting through a licensed operator with certified guides and life jackets — check reviews, not just roadside stalls.
September-October, right after monsoon, offers the biggest and most exciting rapids for rafting.
Attend the Parmarth Niketan evening aarti early to get a front-row seat by the fire pits.
Many ashorams offer short-term yoga and meditation courses — book ahead during the March International Yoga Festival.
Alcohol and meat are officially banned within Rishikesh municipal limits — don't expect bars in town.
thingsToAvoid: Unlicensed rafting operators without visible safety gear or trained guides.
Swimming alone in the Ganges — currents are stronger than they appear, especially post-monsoon.
Overpaying inflated "donation" requests from self-appointed sadhus at the ghats.
Walking on suspension bridges during festival crowds — Laxman Jhula in particular can get dangerously packed.
Bringing alcohol or meat into ashram accommodations — it can get you asked to leave.

name: Amritsar
airportName: Sri Guru Ram Dass Jee International Airport
visaSummary: Most foreign nationals require an Indian e-Visa applied for online before travel. Indian citizens need no visa, and OCI/PIO cardholders travel visa-free with a linked foreign passport — relevant given the very large Punjabi diaspora that visits the Golden Temple regularly.
bestTimeToVisit: October to March, with comfortable highs of 19-27°C for temple visits and the Wagah Border ceremony; avoid May-June when highs exceed 40°C.
weatherSummary: Continental climate typical of Punjab's plains: very hot, dry summers (highs 39-42°C in May-June), a moderate monsoon July-September, and cold, foggy winters (highs 18-20°C, near-freezing nights in January).
etiquette: Cover your head with a scarf or bandana (provided free at the entrance) and remove shoes before entering the Golden Temple complex.
Do not smoke, drink alcohol, or bring leather items into the Golden Temple complex.
Wading in the temple's sacred pool (sarovar) is welcomed for ritual bathing but do so respectfully and modestly.
Accept langar (free community meal) food with both hands as a sign of respect.
Photography is generally allowed at the Golden Temple but avoid photographing worshippers up close without permission.

Suite stay with a Golden Temple view balcony

Fine dining Punjabi tasting menu at a heritage hotel
honeymoonExperiences: Sunrise visit to the Golden Temple together
Evening illumination viewing from the causeway
Quiet walk around Durgiana Temple lake
Rooftop dinner overlooking the old city
budgetExperiences: Free darshan and langar meal at the Golden Temple
Free entry to Jallianwala Bagh and the Wagah Border ceremony
Street food crawl on Hall Bazaar and Kesar Da Dhaba
Walking tour of the old city
localTransportGuide: Amritsar's old city around the Golden Temple is best explored on foot or by cycle-rickshaw; auto-rickshaws and Ola/Uber handle longer distances including the roughly 30km trip to the Wagah Border. Many hotels arrange shared taxis timed for the evening Wagah ceremony.
packingNotes: A scarf or bandana for covering your head at the Golden Temple (also provided free on-site)
Modest clothing — no shorts or sleeveless tops at religious sites
Comfortable shoes for walking on the marble parikrama (barefoot required inside)
Warm layers for cold, foggy winter mornings
travelTips: Visit the Golden Temple before sunrise or after 9pm for the most peaceful experience and best photos with fewer crowds.
Try the free langar meal at least once — sitting cross-legged with strangers from all walks of life is a core part of the experience.
Arrive at the Wagah Border by 3:30-4pm to get a good seat before the 5:15-5:45pm ceremony (timing shifts seasonally).
Amritsari kulcha (stuffed flatbread) with chole is the city's signature breakfast — try it at a local dhaba, not a hotel restaurant.
Leave shoes and socks at the free cloak counters outside the Golden Temple rather than carrying them.
thingsToAvoid: Bringing tobacco, alcohol, or leather goods into the Golden Temple complex.
Skipping the modesty dress code at religious sites — temple volunteers may turn you away.
Unlicensed guides near the Golden Temple charging inflated "mandatory" fees.
Walking barefoot for extended periods on the hot marble parikrama at midday in summer.
Rushing the Wagah Border visit — arrive early since security lines and seating fill up fast.

name: Shimla
airportName: Shimla Airport (small; nearest major gateway: Chandigarh CHD)
visaSummary: Most foreign nationals require an Indian e-Visa applied for online before travel. Indian citizens need no visa, and OCI/PIO cardholders travel visa-free with a linked foreign passport, a popular hill-station combination for NRI family summer trips.
bestTimeToVisit: March to June for pleasant, mild weather (highs 20-25°C) before monsoon, and December-February for a chance of snow; avoid July-August landslide-prone monsoon rains on the mountain roads.
weatherSummary: Temperate Himalayan foothill climate: mild summers (highs 22-25°C, a welcome escape from the plains below), a wet monsoon July-August that can trigger landslides on access roads, and cold winters (highs 8-10°C, with occasional December-January snowfall).
etiquette: Dress warmly and modestly — Shimla is a conservative hill town compared to Indian metros.
Mall Road and the Ridge are pedestrian-only and vehicle-free — respect posted no-vehicle zones.
Remove shoes before entering temples such as Jakhu Temple.
Be cautious and respectful around the resident macaques at Jakhu Temple — do not carry visible food or plastic bags.
Smoking and littering on Mall Road are officially prohibited and can draw fines.

festivals.name: Winter Carnival
festivals.timing: December-January, ice skating and cultural events

Toy train ride together through pine forests
Snowfall walk in Kufri (winter)
budgetExperiences: Free promenade along Mall Road and the Ridge
Budget guesthouse with valley views
Local bus day trip to Kufri
Street snacks (momos, maggi points) around Lower Bazaar
localTransportGuide: Central Shimla (Mall Road, the Ridge) is entirely pedestrian, connected to lower parking areas by lifts and stairways; local taxis and shared cabs handle trips to Kufri, Mashobra and the airport/rail station in lower Shimla. The Kalka-Shimla Toy Train is a scenic (if slow) way to arrive from the plains.
packingNotes: Warm layers year-round — evenings are cool even in summer
A heavy jacket, gloves and waterproof shoes for winter snow
Comfortable shoes for steep pedestrian streets and stairways
Rain gear for the July-August monsoon
travelTips: Book the Kalka-Shimla Toy Train's "Vistadome" or window seats in advance for the best views.
Reach central Shimla by lift from the Cart Road parking level rather than climbing the steep stairs with luggage.
Visit Jakhu Temple early morning to avoid both crowds and the more assertive resident monkeys.
December-January offers a real chance of snowfall in town — pack accordingly if that's the draw.
Landslides can close the Kalka-Shimla highway during heavy monsoon rain — check road conditions if driving in July-August.
thingsToAvoid: Carrying food or plastic bags visibly near Jakhu Temple — monkeys are known to snatch them.
Driving the mountain highway at night or during monsoon landslide warnings.
Overpriced "shawl factory" tour stops arranged by commission-earning taxi drivers.
Wearing open sandals on the steep, sometimes icy stairways of central Shimla in winter.
Assuming toy train tickets are available same-day in peak season — they sell out days ahead.

name: Darjeeling
visaSummary: Most foreign nationals require an Indian e-Visa applied for online before travel; note that a Protected Area Permit is not required for Darjeeling town itself (unlike parts of neighboring Sikkim). Indian citizens need no visa, and OCI/PIO cardholders travel visa-free with a linked foreign passport.
bestTimeToVisit: March to May and October to December for clear Kanchenjunga views and mild highs of 15-20°C; the June-September monsoon brings heavy rain, fog and occasional landslides.
weatherSummary: Cool, temperate hill climate at over 2,000m elevation: mild summers (highs 20-22°C, a relief from the plains), a very wet monsoon June-September with persistent fog and rain, and cold, dry winters (highs 10-12°C, occasional frost).
etiquette: Dress warmly and modestly; many residents are Nepali/Gorkha, Tibetan Buddhist and Bengali — greet with a smile and simple "Namaste".
Ask permission before photographing monks at Ghoom Monastery or workers on tea estate tours.
Remove shoes before entering monastery prayer halls.
Support local tea-estate cooperatives directly rather than only mass-market souvenir shops.
Be respectful and quiet during early morning visits to Tiger Hill — many come specifically for a peaceful sunrise.

festivals.name: Gorkhaland Day observances
festivals.timing: Various, reflecting local Gorkha cultural identity

attractionsPaid.note: UNESCO World Heritage narrow-gauge steam/diesel railway to Ghoom

nightlife: Chowrasta evening promenade with mountain silhouettes
Early bedtime town — most activity centers on pre-dawn Tiger Hill excursions

Tea garden terraces at Happy Valley
hiddenGems: Tukvar Tea Estate quieter alternative to Happy Valley
familyActivities: Rock Garden & Ganga Maya Park picnic
adventureActivities: Trekking toward Sandakphu and Singalila Ridge
Mountain biking through tea estate trails
Paragliding near nearby Sikkim border regions
Pre-dawn Tiger Hill hike for sunrise

Private tea-tasting masterclass at a heritage tea estate
Private toy-train charter carriage to Ghoom
Helicopter sightseeing over the Kanchenjunga range (seasonal)

Sunset walk to the Peace Pagoda

Free wander through Chowrasta and Mall Road
Tea tasting at Nathmull's Tea Room
Budget homestay with tea-garden views
localTransportGuide: Darjeeling town is steep and best explored on foot around Chowrasta and Mall Road; shared jeeps (share taxis) are the standard way to reach Bagdogra airport, Tiger Hill at dawn, and nearby towns like Kalimpong or Mirik. The Darjeeling Himalayan Railway toy train is more a scenic experience than practical transport.
packingNotes: Heavy layers and a warm jacket — mornings are cold even in summer
A woolen hat and gloves for pre-dawn Tiger Hill visits
Waterproof jacket for the June-September monsoon
Sturdy walking shoes for steep, uneven streets
travelTips: Book a shared jeep to Tiger Hill the night before — pickup is typically around 4am for sunrise.
October-December and March-May give the clearest Kanchenjunga views; monsoon months often obscure the peak entirely.
Try tea tastings at multiple estates (Happy Valley and Tukvar) to compare first-flush and second-flush harvests.
The Darjeeling Himalayan Railway's full Kurseong-Darjeeling steam route is more scenic than the shorter joyride to Ghoom.
Landslides can close the hill road from Bagdogra during heavy monsoon rain — build in buffer time.
thingsToAvoid: Expecting guaranteed mountain views — Kanchenjunga is frequently clouded over, especially outside the clear-sky months.
Overpriced "first-flush" tea sold as premium in tourist shops without any estate verification.
Traveling the Siliguri-Darjeeling hill road after dark during monsoon landslide season.
Underestimating the altitude chill — many visitors arrive from hot Kolkata or Delhi underprepared for the cold.
Skipping jeep-sharing etiquette — arrive on time as jeeps leave promptly once full.

name: Leh-Ladakh
visaSummary: Most foreign nationals require an Indian e-Visa applied for online before travel; foreign nationals also need an Inner Line Permit (arranged easily online or on arrival in Leh) to visit areas like Pangong Lake, Nubra Valley and Khardung La. Indian citizens need no visa but do need the same Inner Line Permit for restricted areas; OCI/PIO cardholders travel visa-free with a linked foreign passport.
bestTimeToVisit: Mid-May to September, when the high mountain passes are open and daytime highs reach a comfortable 15-25°C; the region is largely snowbound and inaccessible by road November to April.
weatherSummary: High-altitude cold desert climate (Leh sits at roughly 3,500m): short, mild summers (highs 20-25°C but with strong UV and cold nights), and long, harsh winters (highs near or below freezing, lows dropping to -15°C or colder, with the Zanskar and Indus rivers partly freezing).
etiquette: Acclimatize for at least 24-48 hours in Leh before any exertion or travel to higher passes — altitude sickness is a genuine risk at 3,500m+.
Remove shoes and dress modestly when entering monasteries (gompas); some restrict photography inside prayer halls.
Walk clockwise around Buddhist stupas, prayer wheels and mani walls, as is customary.
Ask permission before photographing monks or local Ladakhi families.
Respect military zones and checkpoints near the Pangong Lake/Nubra Valley border areas — follow permit rules exactly.

festivals.name: Hemis Festival
festivals.timing: June-July, masked Cham dances at Hemis Monastery

attractionsPaid.name: Leh Palace
attractionsPaid.note: Nine-story former royal palace overlooking the old town

attractionsPaid.name: Hemis Monastery
attractionsPaid.note: Largest and wealthiest monastery in Ladakh, home to the Hemis Festival

attractionsFree.note: Small, easily accessible monastery near central Leh

nightlife: Stargazing at Pangong Lake or Nubra Valley campsites (minimal light pollution)

Shanti Stupa at sunset over Leh
hiddenGems: Turtuk village near the Pakistan border
Tso Moriri Lake, quieter than Pangong
familyActivities: Bactrian camel rides in Nubra Valley
adventureActivities: Driving Khardung La, one of the world's highest motorable passes
White-water rafting on the Zanskar/Indus rivers
Mountain biking from Khardung La down to Leh
luxuryExperiences: Luxury glamping at Pangong Lake or Nubra Valley
Private helicopter transfers to remote monasteries (seasonal)
Boutique heritage stay in a restored Ladakhi home
Private jeep safari with a personal guide and photographer
honeymoonExperiences: Sunset at Pangong Lake together
Stargazing night at a Nubra Valley campsite
Private candlelit dinner overlooking the Indus Valley
Sunrise at Shanti Stupa over Leh
budgetExperiences: Shared taxi/group tour to Pangong Lake and Nubra Valley
Free viewpoints at Shanti Stupa and Magnetic Hill
Budget homestay in a Ladakhi family home
Walking tour of Leh's old town and bazaar
localTransportGuide: Leh town is small and walkable, but reaching Pangong Lake, Nubra Valley or Khardung La requires a hired taxi/jeep or an organized tour due to permit requirements and rough mountain roads; self-drive rental motorcycles are hugely popular for the classic Ladakh road trip, but require prior riding experience at altitude.
packingNotes: Warm layers even in summer — nights drop near freezing at altitude
High-SPF sunscreen and UV-protective sunglasses — sun intensity is extreme at altitude
Diamox or altitude-sickness medication after consulting a doctor
A basic first-aid kit and reusable water bottle since bottled water access is limited in remote valleys
travelTips: Spend at least one full acclimatization day in Leh before any activity or altitude gain — rushing to Khardung La or Pangong on day one risks serious altitude sickness.
Arrange Inner Line Permits online in advance or through your hotel/travel agent in Leh; permits are checked at multiple checkpoints.
The road-accessible season is roughly late May to late September — flights operate more reliably outside this window.
Fly into Leh rather than driving in if short on time or acclimatization days — but flying in still requires rest before exertion.
Pangong Lake and Nubra Valley are typically visited via a two-to-three day loop, not as separate day trips, due to distance.
thingsToAvoid: Overexertion (running, heavy luggage carrying, alcohol) within the first 24-48 hours of arrival — altitude sickness can be serious.
Skipping travel insurance that covers high-altitude evacuation.
Traveling without your Inner Line Permit paperwork to restricted zones — checkpoints will turn you back.
Relying on mobile data outside Leh town — network coverage is very limited in Nubra and Pangong.
Underestimating fuel/rest stops on long mountain drives — services are sparse between towns.

name: Andaman Islands
visaSummary: Most foreign nationals require an Indian e-Visa applied for online before travel; foreign nationals also need a free Restricted Area Permit (RAP) for the Andamans, typically issued automatically on arrival at Port Blair airport alongside immigration. Indian citizens need no visa or permit; OCI/PIO cardholders travel visa-free with a linked foreign passport.
bestTimeToVisit: November to April for calm seas, clear skies and the best diving/snorkeling visibility (highs 28-31°C); the May-September monsoon and cyclone season brings rough seas and disrupted ferry schedules.
weatherSummary: Tropical maritime climate: warm and humid year-round (highs 28-31°C), with a monsoon split across two seasons (southwest monsoon June-September, weaker northeast monsoon November-December) that can disrupt inter-island ferries.
etiquette: Respect designated no-swim and coral-protection zones at marine national parks such as Mahatma Gandhi Marine National Park.
Never touch or stand on coral while snorkeling or diving — reef damage here is taken seriously.
Photography inside Cellular Jail's solitary cells is welcomed, but stay quiet and respectful — it is a site of real historical suffering.
Indigenous tribal reserves (Jarawa, Sentinelese) are strictly off-limits — do not attempt to photograph, contact or travel toward tribal reserve areas.
Modest swimwear is appreciated at public beaches shared with local families, versus more remote resort beaches.

attractionsPaid.name: Cellular Jail National Memorial
attractionsPaid.note: Colonial-era prison for freedom fighters, with an evening light-and-sound show

attractionsFree.note: Easily accessible palm-fringed beach near Port Bair

attractionsFree.note: Sunset viewpoint and birdwatching spot at the island's southern tip

attractionsFree.name: Samudrika Naval Marine Museum grounds

attractionsFree.name: Kalapathar Beach, Havelock
attractionsFree.note: Quiet black-rock beach popular at sunrise

nightlife: Beachfront bonfire evenings on Havelock/Neil Island
Stargazing on quieter beaches away from Port Blair
instagramSpots: Kalapathar Beach black rocks at sunrise
hiddenGems: Ross Island ruins overtaken by roots and deer
Baratang limestone caves and mud volcanoes
familyActivities: Glass-bottom boat ride at North Bay Island
adventureActivities: Scuba diving off Havelock and Neil Island
Sea walking at North Bay Island
luxuryExperiences: Overwater-style luxury villa stay at a Havelock beach resort
Private yacht/speedboat island-hopping charter
Private sunset dinner on a secluded beach
PADI advanced dive course with a private instructor
honeymoonExperiences: Sunset at Radhanagar Beach together
Private candlelight dinner on the sand
Couple's snorkeling trip to a quiet reef
Overwater villa stay at a Havelock resort
budgetExperiences: Public ferry (rather than private) between Port Blair and Havelock
Free beach time at Corbyn's Cove and Kalapathar
Budget beach-shack stay on Havelock
Self-guided snorkeling gear rental instead of a guided tour
localTransportGuide: Government and private ferries connect Port Blair to Havelock and Neil Island (book Makruzz or Green Ocean ferries ahead in peak season); within Port Blair, autos and taxis are the norm, while Havelock and Neil are best explored by rented scooter. Inter-island travel by air is limited, so most movement is by sea.
packingNotes: Reef-safe sunscreen to protect the coral
Light, breathable clothing plus swimwear
Water shoes for rocky entry points at some beaches
A dry bag for camera/phone protection on boat transfers
travelTips: Book inter-island ferries to Havelock and Neil Island several days in advance during the November-April peak season.
Foreign nationals should keep their Restricted Area Permit (issued at Port Blair airport) handy — it may be checked at hotels and ferry counters.
Elephant Beach and North Bay have the most accessible snorkeling; serious divers should base in Havelock for the best dive sites.
Radhanagar Beach is best visited in late afternoon for sunset, but arrive with enough daylight left to return before dark.
Cellular Jail's light-and-sound show in the evening adds significant historical context beyond the daytime visit.
thingsToAvoid: Any attempt to visit or photograph the Jarawa or Sentinelese tribal reserves — this is illegal and dangerous.
Touching or standing on coral while snorkeling — it is fragile and slow to recover.
Traveling during cyclone warnings (typically Oct-Dec transition) — ferries and flights can be cancelled with little notice.
Booking only one full day for Havelock — most highlights need at least two nights to enjoy without rushing.
Unlicensed dive operators — verify PADI/SSI certification before booking scuba trips.

name: Colombo
visaSummary: Most foreign nationals, including Indian passport holders, need an Electronic Travel Authorization (ETA) approved online before departure — apply via the official Sri Lanka ETA portal well in advance to avoid airport delays.
bestTimeToVisit: December to March, during the drier northeast monsoon window on the west coast, with warm, humid highs around 29-31°C nearly year-round.
weatherSummary: Tropical climate with little seasonal temperature variation (highs 29-31°C year-round); the southwest monsoon (May-September) brings the heaviest rain to Colombo, while December-March is comparatively dry and sunny.
etiquette: Dress modestly and remove shoes and hats when entering Buddhist and Hindu temples such as Gangaramaya.
Never turn your back to a Buddha statue for a photo — it is considered highly disrespectful.
Use your right hand for eating and giving/receiving items.
Public displays of affection are generally frowned upon.
Bargaining is common in markets like Pettah, but fixed prices are standard in malls and boutiques.

attractionsPaid.note: Eclectic Buddhist temple complex blending multiple architectural styles

City cycling tours through Fort and Pettah

Spa retreat at a five-star Colombo hotel
honeymoonExperiences: Sunset cocktails at Galle Face Hotel
Private dinner at Ministry of Crab
Sunset walk along Galle Face Green
Couple's spa treatment with Ayurvedic influences
budgetExperiences: Street food (hoppers, kottu) crawl at Galle Face Green
Free wander through Pettah Market
Public bus or tuk-tuk city tour
Free entry to Independence Memorial Hall
localTransportGuide: Tuk-tuks (three-wheelers) are the most common way to get around — use the PickMe app for metered, upfront pricing rather than negotiating on the street. Colombo also has a limited suburban rail network and city buses; ride-hailing apps like PickMe and Uber operate reliably in the city.
packingNotes: Lightweight, breathable clothing for year-round heat and humidity
Modest clothing for temple visits (shoulders/knees covered)
A light rain jacket for the May-September monsoon
Sandals suitable for barefoot temple floors
travelTips: Apply for the Sri Lanka ETA online at least a few days before departure — airport-arrival processing can be slow.
Use the PickMe app for tuk-tuks and taxis to avoid price haggling with street drivers.
Galle Face Green is liveliest — and best for street food — right around sunset.
Visit Gangaramaya Temple in the late afternoon to also catch the adjoining Seema Malaka Temple at golden hour.
Pair Colombo with a short trip to Kandy or Negombo — both are under 3 hours by road or rail.
thingsToAvoid: Unmetered tuk-tuk drivers quoting inflated tourist fares — insist on the meter or use PickMe.
Gem "bargain" scams, particularly around Pettah and tourist hotels.
Swimming at Mount Lavinia or Galle Face beaches during rough monsoon surf — currents can be strong.
Photographing military or government installations — this is taken very seriously in Sri Lanka.
Turning your back to a Buddha statue for photos — considered a serious cultural offense.

name: Galle
visaSummary: Most foreign nationals, including Indian passport holders, need an Electronic Travel Authorization (ETA) approved online before arrival into Sri Lanka via Colombo, then travel overland or by rail to Galle.
bestTimeToVisit: December to March for the driest, sunniest weather on the south coast (highs 29-31°C); the southwest monsoon (May-September) brings rougher seas and less reliable beach weather.
weatherSummary: Tropical coastal climate with warm temperatures year-round (highs 29-31°C); the southwest monsoon (May-September) brings the heaviest rain and rougher surf, while December-March is drier with calmer seas ideal for the fort ramparts and nearby beaches.
etiquette: Dress modestly when entering the Meeran Mosque or Sudharmalaya Temple inside the Fort.
Ask permission before photographing residents inside their private courtyards along the Fort's narrow lanes.
Respect quiet hours in residential parts of the Fort, especially in the evenings.
Support Fort artisans and boutique shops directly rather than only mass-produced souvenir stalls.

festivals.name: Vesak Festival
festivals.timing: May (full moon), lantern displays across town

festivals.name: Galle Fort Christmas & New Year celebrations

hiddenGems: Galle Fort's quieter southern ramparts at dawn
adventureActivities: Whale and dolphin watching at Mirissa
Surfing lessons at nearby Weligama or Midigama
Stand-up paddleboarding on calm fort-side waters
luxuryExperiences: Boutique stay at The Fort Printers or Amangalla
Private sunset rampart walk with a historian guide
Fine dining at Church Street Social
Private whale-watching charter from Mirissa
honeymoonExperiences: Sunset at Flag Rock together
Candlelight dinner in a restored Dutch courtyard
Private beach picnic at Jungle Beach
Boutique fort hotel stay with a private plunge pool
budgetExperiences: Free rampart walk around the entire fort
Public bus to Unawatuna Beach
Street food at the Galle bus-stand food stalls
Self-guided colonial architecture walk through the fort lanes
localTransportGuide: Galle Fort itself is entirely walkable and largely car-restricted inside the walls; tuk-tuks (best booked via the PickMe app for fair pricing) cover trips to Unawatuna, Jungle Beach and the bus/train station. The scenic coastal train line connects Galle to Colombo and onward to Mirissa/Matara.
packingNotes: Light, breathable clothing for tropical heat and humidity
A modest cover-up for mosque/temple visits inside the fort
Reef-safe sunscreen for nearby snorkeling beaches
Comfortable sandals for uneven rampart stones and cobbled lanes
travelTips: Walk the fort ramparts at sunset for the best light and cooler temperatures — Flag Rock is the classic spot.
Take the scenic coastal train from Colombo to Galle for ocean views most of the route.
Combine Galle with a Mirissa whale-watching trip (Nov-Apr) — boats depart early morning for the best sightings.
Book fort-wall boutique hotels well ahead in peak season (Dec-Mar); rooms inside the walls are limited.
Unawatuna and Jungle Beach are a short, cheap tuk-tuk ride from the fort — no need to stay directly on the beach.
thingsToAvoid: Swimming at unmarked or unsupervised beaches during monsoon swell (May-Sep) — rip currents are a real risk.
Overpriced "gem" and "sapphire" sales pitches from unlicensed dealers.
Driving/walking on the ramparts edge in wet weather — the stones get slippery.
Booking whale-watching tours with operators that crowd or chase whales — choose responsible-tourism certified operators.
Assuming ATMs are widely available inside the fort — withdraw cash before entering the old town.

name: Kathmandu
visaSummary: Most foreign nationals, including Indian passport holders in most cases, can get a visa on arrival at Tribhuvan International Airport by paying a fee in cash or card and filling an online pre-registration form; Indian citizens actually need no visa at all to enter Nepal (only a valid photo ID), a longstanding open-border arrangement between the two countries.
bestTimeToVisit: October-November and March-April for clear mountain views and comfortable highs of 22-25°C; the June-August monsoon brings heavy rain and obscured Himalayan views.
weatherSummary: Subtropical highland climate moderated by the Kathmandu Valley's elevation (~1,400m): warm summers (highs 28-30°C) with a heavy June-September monsoon, and cool, dry, clear winters (highs 18-20°C, chilly nights near 2-5°C).
etiquette: Remove shoes and dress modestly before entering temples and stupas such as Pashupatinath and Swayambhunath.
Walk clockwise around Buddhist stupas and mani walls.
Non-Hindus are not permitted into the inner sanctum of Pashupatinath Temple, though the surrounding complex can be viewed.
Avoid stepping over people or their food, and use your right hand for eating and giving/receiving items.
Ask permission before photographing cremation ceremonies at Pashupatinath's ghats.

festivals.name: Indra Jatra
festivals.timing: September, chariot processions in Kathmandu Durbar Square

festivals.name: Dashain
festivals.timing: September-October, Nepal's biggest festival (per lunar calendar)

festivals.name: Bisket Jatra
festivals.timing: April, Nepali New Year celebrations in Bhaktapur

localTransportGuide: Taxis and ride-hailing apps like Pathao and InDrive are the easiest way to get around; traffic and pollution can be heavy, so many travelers walk within compact areas like Thamel. Local buses and tempos (shared three-wheelers) serve the wider valley, including Patan and Bhaktapur.
packingNotes: Layered clothing — mornings and evenings are cool even when days are warm
A dust mask or scarf for Kathmandu's traffic pollution
Modest clothing for temple visits
A warm jacket if continuing on to any Himalayan trekking
travelTips: Visit Boudhanath at dawn or dusk when monks and locals do their kora (circumambulation) — it's the most atmospheric time.
Combine Kathmandu, Patan and Bhaktapur Durbar Squares in a single multi-day pass where available to save money.
Indian citizens need only a valid photo ID to enter Nepal — no visa or passport required, though airlines typically still require a passport for international check-in.
Nagarkot, a short drive away, offers one of the best sunrise Himalayan panoramas without a long trek.
Air quality is often poor in Kathmandu in the dry pre-monsoon months (Mar-May) — those with respiratory sensitivity should plan accordingly.
thingsToAvoid: Unlicensed trekking agencies in Thamel offering suspiciously cheap Everest/Annapurna packages — verify TAAN/NTB registration.
Photographing cremation ceremonies at Pashupatinath's ghats.
Drinking tap water — stick to bottled or filtered water.
Overpaying for "antique" Thangka paintings without verifying authenticity.
Underestimating Kathmandu Valley traffic times — short distances can take much longer than expected.

name: Pokhara
visaSummary: Most foreign nationals can get a Nepal visa on arrival (paid in cash or card) at major entry points; Indian citizens need no visa at all to enter Nepal, only a valid photo ID, thanks to the open-border treaty between the two countries.
bestTimeToVisit: October-November and March-April for the clearest views of the Annapurna range and mild highs of 24-28°C; the June-August monsoon frequently obscures mountain views with cloud.
weatherSummary: Subtropical climate at a lower elevation (~820m) than Kathmandu, so noticeably warmer and more humid: hot, wet summers (highs 29-30°C with heavy monsoon rain June-September, as Pokhara is one of Nepal's wettest cities), and mild, drier winters (highs 20-22°C).
etiquette: Dress modestly and remove shoes at temples such as Bindhyabasini and the World Peace Pagoda.
Respect trekking-trail etiquette — yield the uphill side to pack animals (mules/dzos) and greet fellow trekkers with "Namaste".
Support locally-run teahouses and guesthouses on trekking routes rather than only large chains.
Ask permission before photographing porters or guides at work.

localTransportGuide: Lakeside is compact and walkable, with taxis and rented scooters covering trips to Sarangkot or the airport; trekking trailheads (for Annapurna routes) are reached by local jeep or taxi from town. Tourist buses connect Pokhara to Kathmandu (roughly 6-7 hours by road, or a short 25-minute flight).
packingNotes: Layered trekking-friendly clothing given the range of altitudes nearby
Rain gear for the heavy June-September monsoon
Comfortable trekking shoes even for short day hikes to Sarangkot
Sunglasses and sun protection for high-altitude viewpoints
travelTips: Head to Sarangkot before dawn for the best chance of a clear, cloud-free Annapurna sunrise.
Book paragliding for the morning — winds tend to pick up and visibility can drop in the afternoon.
Pokhara is the standard starting point for the Annapurna Circuit and Poon Hill treks — arrange permits (TIMS card, ACAP) in town.
The 25-minute flight from Kathmandu saves a full day versus the winding mountain road.
Begnas Lake offers a quieter, less touristy alternative to Phewa Lake for a half-day escape.
thingsToAvoid: Booking cut-rate trekking guides without proper licensing or insurance for high-altitude routes.
Paragliding with unlicensed operators — verify APPI/PPA certification.
Expecting mountain views during the monsoon (Jun-Sep) — cloud cover is the norm, not the exception.
Swimming in Phewa Lake far from designated areas — depths and currents vary.
Underestimating altitude effects if continuing straight onto a high trek without acclimatization.

name: Paro, Bhutan
visaSummary: Most foreign nationals must arrange a Bhutan visa in advance through a licensed Bhutanese tour operator (or an operator-affiliated agent abroad), and pay the mandatory Sustainable Development Fee (SDF) as part of their trip cost. Indian, Bangladeshi and Maldivian nationals get simplified entry using just a passport or voter ID (no visa fee), but Indian nationals are still liable for a reduced SDF (roughly USD 15 per person per night as of recent policy) payable locally — a significant and directly relevant nuance for Indian travelers who otherwise assume Bhutan is "visa-free and fee-free" like Nepal.
bestTimeToVisit: March-May and September-November for clear Himalayan views, mild highs of 15-20°C and major festivals (tshechus); winter (Dec-Feb) is cold but offers crisp mountain visibility with fewer crowds.
weatherSummary: Cool temperate climate shaped by elevation (~2,200m in Paro Valley): mild summers (highs 22-24°C) with a monsoon June-August, and cold winters (highs 10-12°C, frequent frost and occasional snow at higher elevations).
etiquette: Dress modestly and remove hats when entering dzongs (fortress-monasteries) and temples; a formal covering "kabney/rachu" scarf may be provided for certain ceremonial visits.
Walk clockwise around chortens, prayer wheels and monasteries.
Remove shoes before entering temple halls and always follow your licensed guide's instructions inside sacred spaces.
Photography is often prohibited inside temple sanctums — always ask your guide before taking pictures.
Smoking is banned in most public places nationwide, and the sale of tobacco is tightly restricted.

Private helicopter transfer for panoramic Himalayan views
Personalized guided cultural immersion with a private guide-driver team (standard under Bhutan's tourism model)
Traditional hot-stone bath (dotsho) spa ritual
honeymoonExperiences: Sunrise hike to Tiger's Nest together
Private candlelight dinner at a luxury valley resort
Couple's traditional hot-stone bath ritual
Private archery lesson followed by a farmhouse picnic
budgetExperiences: Note: Bhutan's tourism model requires all visitors (except regional nationals) to book through a licensed operator, so a true "budget" trip means selecting a modest-tier package rather than independent budget travel
Homestay accommodation through your licensed operator
Packed picnic lunches instead of resort dining, arranged via your operator
Group tour packages to share guide/driver costs
parks: Paro Valley farmland and river corridor
Jigme Dorji National Park (accessible from the Paro region)
localTransportGuide: Independent travel is not really the norm in Bhutan — nearly all visitors move by private car or van with their licensed operator's driver and guide, which is built into the standard tour package. Paro town itself is small and easily walkable.
packingNotes: Warm layers year-round — valley evenings are cool even in summer
Sturdy hiking shoes for the Tiger's Nest trail
Modest, respectful clothing for dzong and temple visits (long sleeves/trousers)
A reusable water bottle and rain layer for the Jun-Aug monsoon
travelTips: Book your trip through a licensed Bhutanese tour operator well in advance — this arranges your visa, guide, driver, hotels and the Sustainable Development Fee in one package.
Start the Tiger's Nest hike early morning to avoid midday heat and afternoon cloud buildup that can obscure views.
Indian nationals should confirm the current Sustainable Development Fee rate for regional visitors before booking, as it differs from the standard international SDF.
Bhutan's "high-value, low-impact" tourism policy means fewer crowds at major sites than comparable Himalayan destinations — pace your visit to enjoy the quiet.
Attending a tshechu (masked dance festival) if your dates align is one of the most memorable cultural experiences in the country.
thingsToAvoid: Assuming Bhutan can be visited independently without a licensed operator — most nationalities cannot arrange this on arrival.
Underestimating the Tiger's Nest hike — it is steep and at altitude; those with mobility or fitness concerns can rent a mule partway.
Skipping travel insurance — medical evacuation from remote valleys can be costly.
Littering or plastic bag use — Bhutan enforces environmental regulations strictly, including a nationwide plastic bag ban.
Photographing inside temple sanctums without your guide's explicit confirmation it is allowed.

name: Miami
visaSummary: Citizens of Visa Waiver Program countries (including most of Western Europe, UK, Australia, Japan) can enter with an approved ESTA for stays up to 90 days. Other nationalities, including Indian passport holders, need a B1/B2 visitor visa obtained in advance through a US embassy/consulate interview — a key planning consideration for this site's Indian traveler audience, since processing/interview wait times can run into months.
bestTimeToVisit: December to April for warm, dry, comfortable weather (highs 24-28°C) and lower hurricane risk; June-November is Atlantic hurricane season with hot, humid, storm-prone conditions.
weatherSummary: Tropical monsoon climate: hot, humid summers (highs 31-33°C, near-daily afternoon thunderstorms) and Atlantic hurricane season June-November, with warm, drier and more comfortable winters (highs 24-26°C).
etiquette: Tipping 18-20% at restaurants is standard and expected, not optional.
South Beach and Art Deco district have relaxed swimwear norms right on the sand, but cover up when entering restaurants and shops.
Spanish is widely spoken, especially in Little Havana — a friendly "gracias" is always appreciated.
Nightclub dress codes are enforced (no flip-flops, athletic wear) at most South Beach venues.

localTransportGuide: Miami is car-dependent overall, but South Beach and downtown are walkable, with the free Metromover downtown people-mover and Uber/Lyft covering the rest. Renting a car is recommended for day trips to the Everglades or Key West.
packingNotes: Lightweight, breathable summer clothing year-round
A rain jacket for near-daily afternoon summer thunderstorms
Reef-safe sunscreen and swimwear
A smart-casual outfit for South Beach nightclub dress codes
travelTips: Book Everglades airboat tours for the morning — wildlife is more active and it's cooler before midday heat.
December-April is peak season with the best weather and highest hotel prices; book well ahead for Art Basel week in early December.
Parking in South Beach is expensive and limited — consider rideshare instead of a rental car for that specific area.
Cuban coffee (cafecito) at a Little Havana walk-up window is a quintessential, cheap local experience.
Track hurricane forecasts if traveling June-November — the season peaks in August-October.
thingsToAvoid: Renting a car solely for South Beach — parking is scarce and pricey; use it only for out-of-town day trips.
Swimming during Atlantic hurricane season without checking rip current/beach flag warnings.
Skipping travel/medical insurance — US healthcare costs for foreign visitors can be extremely high.
Timeshare-presentation "free gift" pitches on Ocean Drive and near tourist attractions.
Underestimating summer humidity — outdoor sightseeing is best done morning or evening June-September.

name: Los Angeles
visaSummary: Citizens of Visa Waiver Program countries can enter with an approved ESTA for stays up to 90 days. Other nationalities, including Indian passport holders, require a B1/B2 visitor visa obtained in advance via a US embassy/consulate interview — plan several weeks to months ahead given typical interview wait times.
bestTimeToVisit: March to May and September to November for mild, dry, uncrowded weather (highs 22-26°C); summer (Jun-Aug) is warm and busiest with tourists despite coastal "June gloom" morning cloud.
weatherSummary: Mediterranean climate with mild, dry conditions nearly year-round: warm summers (highs 27-29°C, virtually no rain), and mild winters (highs 18-20°C) with most of the region's modest annual rainfall concentrated December-March.
etiquette: Tipping 18-20% at restaurants and for rideshare/service is standard practice.
Celebrity-spotting is common but approaching for photos without consent is considered rude and can draw security intervention.
Jaywalking is technically illegal and can draw a fine in some areas, though widely done by locals.
Studio tours and sets often have strict no-photography rules in certain areas — follow posted signage.

localTransportGuide: LA is famously car-centric and sprawling — renting a car is the most practical option for most visitors, though Metro rail lines (like the E Line to Santa Monica) and Uber/Lyft cover key tourist corridors. Traffic is heavy at nearly all hours, so budget extra time between neighborhoods.
packingNotes: Layers for cool mornings/evenings and warm afternoons
Sunscreen and a hat for near-constant sun
Comfortable walking shoes for hiking Griffith Park or Runyon Canyon
A light jacket for coastal "June gloom" fog
travelTips: Rent a car if visiting multiple neighborhoods — public transit coverage is limited relative to the city's size.
Visit Griffith Observatory at sunset for the best Hollywood Sign photos and city-light views afterward.
Book Universal Studios or Disneyland tickets online in advance with a specific date to secure lower pricing and entry time slots.
Santa Monica and Venice Beach are best combined into one beach-day itinerary via the beachfront bike path.
Traffic peaks roughly 7-10am and 4-7pm — schedule cross-town drives outside these windows when possible.
thingsToAvoid: Driving during rush hour between distant neighborhoods (e.g., Downtown to Santa Monica) without buffer time.
Star-map and celebrity-home-tour street vendors selling outdated or inaccurate maps near Hollywood Boulevard.
Leaving valuables visible in parked rental cars — break-ins are a known issue at trailheads and beach parking lots.
Assuming walkability between attractions — distances that look short on a map can be a long, unwalkable drive.
Skipping sunscreen — the UV index stays high even on cool or overcast days.

name: San Francisco
visaSummary: Citizens of Visa Waiver Program countries can enter with an approved ESTA for stays up to 90 days. Other nationalities, including Indian passport holders, require a B1/B2 visitor visa obtained in advance via a US embassy/consulate interview.
bestTimeToVisit: September to November for the city's warmest, clearest weather (highs 20-23°C, after summer fog clears); summer (Jun-Aug) is notoriously cool and foggy despite being "peak season" elsewhere.
weatherSummary: Cool Mediterranean/maritime climate: mild, often foggy summers (highs just 18-19°C due to the marine layer, famously colder than spring/fall) and mild, wetter winters (highs 13-14°C, most of the year's rain falls Nov-Mar).
etiquette: Tipping 18-20% at restaurants is standard.
Be aware some downtown/Tenderloin blocks have visible homelessness — stay alert but respectful, and avoid judgmental behavior.
Cable cars and steep hills mean comfortable shoes are a practical necessity, not just a style choice.
San Francisco is generally casual — smart-casual dress is fine at most restaurants, even upscale ones.

localTransportGuide: San Francisco is compact and walkable but very hilly — the Muni system (buses, streetcars, cable cars) and BART regional rail cover most tourist areas well, and a Clipper card works across all of them. Rideshare apps (Uber/Lyft, both founded here) are widely used for hill-heavy or late-night trips.
packingNotes: Layers, including a warm jacket — "summer" can feel like winter due to fog
Comfortable, sturdy shoes for steep hills
A windbreaker for the often-breezy waterfront
Sunglasses for the clearer fall months
travelTips: Pack warm layers regardless of season — the famous fog means summer days can be cooler than winter ones.
Visit Alcatraz early morning or evening for a less crowded ferry and cooler walking conditions on the island.
September-October, after fog season eases, is the best-kept-secret time to visit for clear, warm weather.
Ride the cable car on the Powell-Hyde line for the best hill views down to the bay.
Combine a day trip to Muir Woods with Sausalito for redwoods plus a scenic Bay ferry ride back.
thingsToAvoid: The Tenderloin and parts of the Mid-Market area late at night — exercise normal urban caution.
Driving Lombard Street at peak midday — long queues form; walk it instead for a better experience.
Underdressing for "summer" — many visitors are caught off guard by the cold, foggy microclimate.
Leaving valuables visible in parked rental cars — break-ins at tourist parking areas (like near Golden Gate Bridge viewpoints) are common.
Skipping advance Alcatraz tickets — tours regularly sell out days or weeks ahead in peak season.

name: Chicago
visaSummary: Citizens of Visa Waiver Program countries can enter with an approved ESTA for stays up to 90 days. Other nationalities, including Indian passport holders, require a B1/B2 visitor visa obtained in advance via a US embassy/consulate interview.
bestTimeToVisit: May-June and September-October for mild, comfortable weather and the city's famous outdoor festival season; January-February brings brutally cold "Windy City" winters (highs often below freezing).
weatherSummary: Continental climate with dramatic seasonal swings: hot, humid summers (highs 27-30°C, occasional heat waves) and very cold, windy winters (highs frequently 0-2°C or below, with significant lake-effect snow off Lake Michigan).
etiquette: Tipping 18-20% at restaurants and bars is standard.
Chicagoans take deep-dish vs. thin-crust pizza loyalty seriously — a fun, low-stakes local debate to know about.
Dress warmly and practically in winter — the "Windy City" wind chill is a real factor, not just a nickname.
Public transit (the "L" train) etiquette expects giving up priority seating for elderly/disabled riders.

attractionsPaid.name: Willis Tower Skydeck
attractionsPaid.note: Glass-floor observation deck on the 103rd floor

attractionsPaid.name: 360 CHICAGO (formerly John Hancock Observatory)

attractionsPaid.name: Art Institute of Chicago
attractionsPaid.note: World-class art museum home to "American Gothic" and Impressionist masterpieces

attractionsPaid.name: Shedd Aquarium
attractionsPaid.note: One of the largest indoor aquariums in the world, on the lakefront

attractionsPaid.name: Field Museum of Natural History
attractionsPaid.note: Home to "Sue," the largest and most complete T. rex skeleton found

attractionsPaid.name: Adler Planetarium

Tasting menu at Alinea, a three-Michelin-star restaurant
Private architecture river cruise charter
VIP rooftop lounge access overlooking the skyline
